name: Frankie Dunlop
abstract: Francis Dunlop (December 6, 1928 – July 7, 2014) was an American jazz drummer. Dunlop, born in Buffalo, New York, grew up in a musical family and began playing piano at age nine and drums at ten.

concerts.label: Jack Humphrey Septet/ Maynard Ferguson and His Orchestra/ Sarah Vaughan/ Modern Jazz Quartet
Midnight Performance: Jack Humphrey Septet/ Maynard Ferguson and His Orchestra/ Sarah Vaughan/ Modern Jazz Quartet
Benefit Concert for the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee
Jimmy Smith, Oscar Peterson Trio, and Lou Rawls with the Frankie Dunlop Trio
Kool Jazz Festival: Musicians for Monk

about_quotes.speaker: Byron Stripling
about_quotes.evidence: I got to go on a six week tour of Europe with Lionel Hampton’s band, a guy named Frankie Dunlop was the drummer... It was the first time I realized that a drum could be a melodic instrument.
about_quotes.doc_title: Byron Stripling interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 25, 1998 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Carmen Leggio
about_quotes.evidence: I played with my own group at the Overseas Press Club in New York and Frank Dunlop was on that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Carmen Leggio interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Wendell Brunious
about_quotes.evidence: Well he paid weekly, very weakly. But it was okay. You know there were some great guys in the band. Curtis Fuller was in the band, Ricky Ford, Frankie Dunlop played drums.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wendell Brunious interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, April 16, 2000 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: And among the faculty were Frankie Dunlop, the drummer who played with Thelonious Monk... And so I was very lucky... I met him there
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Jason Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: He talked a lot about Thelonious Monk’s music and how he was really checking that out and what he was learning from Frankie Dunlop.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jason Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Winard Harper
about_quotes.evidence: him and Higgins both had a big influence on me as well as Elvin and a bunch of other guys, Frankie Dunlop, Billy Hart.
about_quotes.doc_title: Winard Harper interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, October 10, 2014
